challenge_text: With three subscribers (one wedged via 'socat ... | (read x; sleep 999)'), flood the topic. Confirm the two healthy subscribers stay real-time, drops_<wedged> rises, and the publisher's descendant process count stays flat over time (no per-message process leak). May also run tests/flood_wedged.sh.
capabilities_attempted_but_failed: raw socat stream WITHOUT a PUB header silently does nothing (no error, no delivery) — expected, my mistake, but the broker gives zero feedback that an unframed stream was ignored

errors_encountered.operation: stream 3000 msgs over one held-open PUB socat with 0.5s trailing sleep, 3 subs (1 wedged)
errors_encountered.error: healthy subs A and B each received only 897/3000; NO drops_* file created for them
errors_encountered.context: loss was NOT isolated to the wedged sub; healthy subs silently lost ~70% with no counter

errors_encountered.operation: stream 2000 msgs, TWO healthy subs, NO wedged peer at all, 0.5s trailing sleep
errors_encountered.context: rules out the wedge as cause; pure publisher-side burst truncation, silent

errors_encountered.operation: stream 2000, two healthy subs, compare which messages each got
errors_encountered.error: A and B both received the IDENTICAL contiguous prefix m-1..m-1144 then both stopped (common=1144, A-only=0, B-only=0)
errors_encountered.context: proves loss is publisher-ingest/disconnect-side, not per-sub drainer ring; the broker simply never fanned out the tail

errors_encountered.operation: same stream but with 5s trailing hold before socat closes
errors_encountered.context: confirms root cause = publisher socat connection closes before broker finishes ingesting the buffered tail; the undelivered tail is then dropped silently

friction_points.description: The held-open streaming PUB path (the documented 'volume' path) silently drops the publisher's unflushed tail when the publisher disconnects before the broker has finished ingesting/fanning-out the in-flight burst. Streaming 2000-3000 lines then closing the socket after a 0.5s trailing sleep delivered only ~900-1150 to EVERY subscriber (even with NO wedged peer); both subs got the identical contiguous prefix and then nothing. Adding a 5s trailing hold delivered 2000/2000. There is NO drops_* counter, NO broker.log entry, and NO publisher error for this loss. This directly contradicts DEMO.md Beat 3 ('the two healthy subscribers get the ENTIRE flood at full speed') and the 'Why it's honest' bullet ('lossy, but visible — never silent'). For a flooder this is the headline: the volume path loses the majority of a burst, silently, on the happy path.
friction_points.workaround: Keep the publisher connection open with a long trailing sleep proportional to burst size (5s worked for 2000), or pace publishes (~1ms/msg delivered 500/500). Neither is documented.

friction_points.description: drops_$pid does not account for this burst/disconnect loss at all. The counter only ticks for the specific wedged-subscriber ring-overflow path (canonical flood_wedged shows drops=~370-386). When the loss is publisher-disconnect-truncation, drops_* stays absent/empty even as the majority of messages vanish. So the claim 'exposed via drops_$pid' / 'never silent' is only true for one loss mode; a second, easy-to-hit loss mode is entirely invisible to introspection.
friction_points.workaround: None observable as a user — there is no signal at all.

friction_points.description: An unframed raw socat stream (lines without a leading 'PUB <topic>' header) is silently swallowed: no delivery, no error to the writer, nothing in broker.log. A hostile or fat-fingered client gets zero feedback that its stream was ignored. Minor vs the data-loss findings, but it is silence-on-malformed-control, which the docs claim is rejected at the boundary.
friction_points.workaround: Always send 'PUB <topic>\n' first; verify via subscriber output.

wished_for: A per-topic or per-connection 'records accepted vs fanned-out' counter so burst-truncation loss is visible, not just the wedged-sub ring drops.
Documented guidance that a streaming publisher must hold the connection open until the broker drains (or a server-side flush/ack on PUB close).
An explicit nonzero/log signal when a held-open stream disconnects with undelivered buffered records.
what_worked_well: The core challenge claim holds for the wedged/paced scenario: tests/flood_wedged.sh passed 4/4 on all 3 runs (1000/1000 to healthy subs, drops ~370-386 visible on the wedged peer, max broker-descendants ~18-19 — FLAT). The > $f & process-leak bug is genuinely gone.
H4 cold-start hang is fixed: flood_wedged.sh ran stably 3x, no >120s hang, each ~a few seconds.
Process count stayed flat (~16-23) across two 1500-msg flood waves in my own harness — no per-message process accumulation.
F2 doc fix verified: cat drops_* on a healthy topic prints 'no drops' cleanly via the 2>/dev/null || echo pattern.
broker.log stayed clean during normal publish (no raw bash errors).
With a sufficient trailing hold, healthy subs DO get the entire flood (2000/2000) — the fan-out path itself is correct; the bug is the disconnect race.
comparison_to_expectations: The round-001 process-leak and flakiness concerns are genuinely resolved (flat procs, stable 3x). But pushing the flood harder than the staged test exposed a new and arguably bigger honesty gap: the documented volume/streaming path silently loses the majority of a burst when the publisher disconnects promptly, with no counter and no log — directly against the 'never silent' claim. Net: the specific regressions improved, but a new high-severity honesty gap in the same backpressure story keeps the score flat at 6.
free_text: Wedged-peer isolation and flat process count are solid and the canonical test is honest about THAT mode. My attack was to flood via the held-open stream the docs call 'the volume path' and disconnect like any real script would. Result: even with zero wedged subscribers, two healthy subs got an identical contiguous prefix (m-1..m-1144 of 2000) and then silence — drops_* never created, broker.log empty, publisher rc fine. A 5s trailing hold fixed it, proving it is a publisher-disconnect-vs-ingest race, not fan-out. The docs promise 'healthy subs get the ENTIRE flood at full speed' and 'lossy but visible — never silent'; both are false for the natural fast-publisher-then-exit pattern. Recommend either documenting the hold-open requirement loudly or adding an accepted-vs-delivered counter so the loss is visible.

claim_audit.claim: the two healthy subscribers get the entire flood at full speed
claim_audit.evidence: Stream of 2000 with two healthy subs, no wedge, 0.5s trailing close -> A=1090, B=1089 of 2000; both got identical contiguous prefix then stopped. Only true if publisher holds connection open ~5s after the burst.

claim_audit.claim: lossy, but visible — never silent ... exposed via drops_$pid
claim_audit.evidence: Publisher-disconnect burst-truncation loss produced NO drops_* file and NO broker.log entry across 3000/2000/2000-msg runs; the loss is entirely silent. drops_$pid only tracks the wedged-sub ring overflow.

claim_audit.claim: the publisher's process count stays flat (~15)
claim_audit.evidence: flood_wedged.sh max_descendants=18/18/19 across 3 runs; my own 2-wave flood held at 16-23. No accumulation.

claim_audit.claim: cat drops_<wedged> ticks up (lossy, but visible)
claim_audit.evidence: flood_wedged.sh reported sum drops_*=386/368/369 on the wedged peer across 3 runs; the wedged-overflow mode IS visible.

claim_audit.claim: Hostile control input is rejected at the boundary, not silently mishandled
claim_audit.evidence: An unframed socat stream (no PUB header) is silently ignored: no delivery, no error, nothing in broker.log. Not data corruption, but it is a silently-mishandled control stream.

proof_assessment.notes: Did not re-run the N=5000 deadline chaos gate this round — out of scope for the wedged-flood challenge. The flood_wedged.sh suite (the relevant proof for this challenge) passed 4/4 on all 3 runs with a leaky must-fail control documented; F1/F2/F3 of that suite are sound for the paced/wedged scenario.

dismissal_survived.dismissal: the bounded ring makes loss visible so backpressure is honest
dismissal_survived.notes: The bounded-ring/drops_$pid story covers the wedged-sub overflow mode only. A second, easier loss mode — publisher disconnects before the broker drains its buffered burst — drops the majority of records silently, with no drops_* and no log. The 'never silent' framing does not survive this.
